data.title: Evercore Private Markets Forum Highlights Push Into Broader Advisory Fee Pools
data.description:
• Evercore (NYSE:EVR) plans to host its third annual Private Markets Forum in New York.
  • The event will bring together senior leaders from around 20 private markets asset management firms.
  • Discussions are expected to focus on trends, risks, and opportunities in private capital markets.

Evercore, an independent investment banking advisory firm, is using this forum to engage directly with major private markets managers at a time when private capital continues to play a central role in global deal activity. For you as an investor, the event highlights how NYSE:EVR is positioning its advisory franchise around fee pools that sit outside traditional public markets.

While the forum itself does not change Evercore’s financials, it offers a window into topics that may influence deal structures, capital formation, and client priorities over time. Following the discussion themes from this gathering can help you understand where private capital participants are focusing attention and how an advisor such as Evercore seeks to stay relevant to that flow of activity.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Evercore by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Evercore.

We've flagged 1 risk for Evercore. See which could impact your investment.

The Private Markets Forum points directly to where Evercore is trying to deepen its advisory relevance, in areas like private equity, private credit, and other alternative assets that sit outside listed markets. For you, the key takeaway is that Evercore is not only focused on traditional M&A; it is also leaning into conversations about fund-raising, capital deployment, and secondary transactions that private managers care about. That can influence how it shapes services in private capital advisory, restructuring, and related fee pools, especially as competition from firms such as Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and Lazard remains intense.

How This Fits Into The Evercore Narrative

  • The forum aligns with the existing narrative that Evercore is leaning more into private capital advisory and complex mandates, which can support the idea of more diversified revenue streams over time.
  • At the same time, it highlights execution risk, because success depends on converting relationship-building and thought leadership at these events into actual mandates across cycles.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on deal activity and international expansion, while this forum underlines Evercore’s effort to influence how private capital itself is structured, which may not be fully reflected in earlier assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Evercore to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Evercore is investing time and resources in private markets engagement, and if deal flows or fundraising slow, the return on that effort could be limited.
  • ⚠️ Competition for private capital advisory work from global banks and independent advisors could pressure fees and make it harder to stand out, even with high profile forums.
  • 🎁 Deepening ties with around 20 large private markets managers can reinforce Evercore’s role on complex transactions and potentially support more recurring advisory work.
  • 🎁 The forum gives Evercore insight into evolving risks and structures in private markets, which can help it tailor products and advice in ways that resonate with clients.

What To Watch Going Forward

After this forum, watch for any commentary from Evercore on how private capital conversations are shaping its advisory pipeline, especially in private capital advisory, restructuring, and secondary transactions. It is also worth tracking how the firm talks about private markets on the upcoming Q1 2026 earnings call, including whether client engagement from events like this is translating into mandates, cross border deals, or new fund related assignments.

Approach 1: Woodward Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today. For Woodward, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections rather than earnings.

Woodward's latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about $430.8 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections suggest free cash flow of $339.1 million in 2026, rising through the forecast period to $1,079.3 million in 2035, with each year discounted back to today's dollars. All figures are in US$.

When these projected cash flows are aggregated, the model indicates an estimated intrinsic value of about $299.04 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $394.83, this DCF view suggests the stock is around 32.0% above that estimated intrinsic value, based on the current set of assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Woodward Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Woodward, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of current earnings, which helps you compare it with other profitable businesses.

What counts as a reasonable P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually points to a lower, more cautious multiple.

Woodward currently trades on a P/E of 48.16x. That sits above both the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 39.61x and the peer group average of 57.10x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Woodward is 30.68x. This is the P/E level it might trade on given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific fundamentals rather than assuming all firms should trade on similar multiples. Comparing 48.16x with the Fair Ratio of 30.68x points to Woodward trading meaningfully above that benchmark.

data.title: It Might Not Be A Great Idea To Buy HKFoods Oyj (HEL:HKFOODS) For Its Next Dividend
data.description:
Some investors rely on dividends for growing their wealth, and if you're one of those dividend sleuths, you might be intrigued to know that HKFoods Oyj (HEL:HKFOODS) is about to go ex-dividend in just three days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Thus, you can purchase HKFoods Oyj's shares before the 23rd of April in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 4th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is €0.08 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of €0.08 per share to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, HKFoods Oyj has a trailing yield of 4.2% on the current stock price of €1.898. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether HKFoods Oyj's dividend is reliable and sustainable. So we need to investigate whether HKFoods Oyj can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Last year, HKFoods Oyj paid out 104% of its income as dividends, which is above a level that we're comfortable with, especially if the company needs to reinvest in its business. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Thankfully its dividend payments took up just 48% of the free cash flow it generated, which is a comfortable payout ratio.

It's disappointing to see that the dividend was not covered by profits, but cash is more important from a dividend sustainability perspective, and HKFoods Oyj fortunately did generate enough cash to fund its dividend. Still, if the company repeatedly paid a dividend greater than its profits, we'd be concerned. Very few companies are able to sustainably pay dividends larger than their reported earnings.

View our latest analysis for HKFoods Oyj

Click here to see how much of its profit HKFoods Oyj paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

When earnings decline, dividend companies become much harder to analyse and own safely. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. Readers will understand then, why we're concerned to see HKFoods Oyj's earnings per share have dropped 17% a year over the past five years. When earnings per share fall, the maximum amount of dividends that can be paid also falls.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. HKFoods Oyj has seen its dividend decline 5.4% per annum on average over the past 10 years, which is not great to see. It's never nice to see earnings and dividends falling, but at least management has cut the dividend rather than potentially risk the company's health in an attempt to maintain it.

Final Takeaway

Is HKFoods Oyj worth buying for its dividend? It's not a great combination to see a company with earnings in decline and paying out 104% of its profits, which could imply the dividend may be at risk of being cut in the future. Yet cashflow was much stronger, which makes us wonder if there are some large timing issues in HKFoods Oyj's cash flows, or perhaps the company has written down some assets aggressively, reducing its income. It's not an attractive combination from a dividend perspective, and we're inclined to pass on this one for the time being.

With that being said, if you're still considering HKFoods Oyj as an investment, you'll find it beneficial to know what risks this stock is facing. Every company has risks, and we've spotted 1 warning sign for HKFoods Oyj you should know about.

data.title: Weak Statutory Earnings May Not Tell The Whole Story For Al Ashghal Al Moysra (TADAWUL:9608)
data.description:
The subdued market reaction suggests that Al Ashghal Al Moysra Co.'s (TADAWUL:9608) recent earnings didn't contain any surprises. Our analysis suggests that along with soft profit numbers, investors should be aware of some other underlying weaknesses in the numbers.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Al Ashghal Al Moysra's Earnings

Many investors haven't heard of the accrual ratio from cashflow, but it is actually a useful measure of how well a company's profit is backed up by free cash flow (FCF) during a given period. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

For the year to December 2025, Al Ashghal Al Moysra had an accrual ratio of 0.24. Unfortunately, that means its free cash flow fell significantly short of its reported profits. Even though it reported a profit of ر.س5.01m, a look at free cash flow indicates it actually burnt through ر.س6.0m in the last year. We also note that Al Ashghal Al Moysra's free cash flow was actually negative last year as well, so we could understand if shareholders were bothered by its outflow of ر.س6.0m.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Al Ashghal Al Moysra.

Our Take On Al Ashghal Al Moysra's Profit Performance

Al Ashghal Al Moysra didn't convert much of its profit to free cash flow in the last year, which some investors may consider rather suboptimal. Therefore, it seems possible to us that Al Ashghal Al Moysra's true underlying earnings power is actually less than its statutory profit. Sadly, its EPS was down over the last twelve months. Of course, we've only just scratched the surface when it comes to analysing its earnings; one could also consider margins, forecast growth, and return on investment, among other factors. Keep in mind, when it comes to analysing a stock it's worth noting the risks involved. Our analysis shows 4 warning signs for Al Ashghal Al Moysra (2 are a bit concerning!) and we strongly recommend you look at them before investing.

  • Both transactions are described as meaningful for Gilead's cell therapy and cancer treatment ambitions.

For investors, these deals highlight how Gilead is leaning further into oncology alongside its established presence in antiviral therapies and other serious diseases. Large biopharma groups have been active in cell therapy and antibody drug conjugates, reflecting interest in targeted cancer treatments. Within that context, Gilead's latest moves indicate where management is choosing to allocate capital inside the broader drug development cycle.

Key questions relate to execution, including clinical progress, safety profiles, and how these assets integrate with Gilead's existing platforms. For Gilead, closing the US$7.8b Arcellx deal and moving ahead with the Tubulis acquisition point to a clear push to balance its HIV and antiviral base with higher risk, oncology-focused assets. Arcellx brings cell therapies that sit closer to Gilead’s Kite franchise, while Tubulis adds antibody drug conjugate technology that competes in the same broad arena as AstraZeneca, Daiichi Sankyo, Roche and others. Together, these moves concentrate more capital in areas where clinical execution, safety outcomes and manufacturing reliability often decide whether a program becomes a franchise or stays a niche product. Investors now have more of Gilead’s future tied to complex oncology platforms that can require substantial follow-on spending for trials, production facilities and commercial build out. On the other hand, management is addressing long term questions about how to supplement HIV cash flows, especially with policy and patent risks already on the radar. How quickly Arcellx and Tubulis assets progress through trials, and how they compete against therapies from companies such as Bristol Myers Squibb, Novartis and Merck, are likely to be closely watched as part of Gilead’s broader repositioning in cancer care.

  • The size of the Arcellx acquisition and the added R&D commitment for Tubulis highlight execution and integration risks that already feature as concerns around pipeline diversification and market access.
  • The specific clinical profiles, timelines and potential competition for the acquired assets are not fully captured in the higher level focus on new launches and broader oncology expansion in the current narrative.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to follow closing of the Arcellx tender offer, any updated guidance on deal related spending, and early commentary on how Arcellx and Tubulis will slot into Gilead’s existing oncology and cell therapy operations. Clinical milestones, safety updates, and competitive data from peers such as Bristol Myers Squibb, Novartis and Merck will help show whether these assets can stand out in crowded cancer indications. data.title: Astronics Qi2 Wireless Charging Launch Tests Cabin Power Growth Story
data.description:
• Astronics (NasdaqGS:ATRO) has introduced its new EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module for aircraft cabins, galleys, and crew rest areas.
  • The module supports the latest Qi enabled and MagSafe compatible devices and is designed for rapid maintenance and compliance with aviation standards.

Astronics focuses on aerospace and defense electronics, with cabin power and connectivity as a core part of its business. The EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module fits directly into that franchise and aims to address airline and passenger demand for reliable onboard power as more devices rely on wireless charging.

For investors following NasdaqGS:ATRO, this product launch adds another building block to the company’s aircraft cabin systems portfolio. As airlines refresh cabins and look to standardize charging for a wider range of devices, adoption and feedback on this module may help indicate how Astronics’ technology roadmap aligns with customer priorities.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Astronics by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Astronics.

We've flagged 2 risks for Astronics. See which could impact your investment.

The EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module looks like a logical extension of Astronics’ in-seat power footprint rather than a standalone bet. By supporting 25W charging, Qi2 and MagSafe compatibility, and integration into seats, galleys, and crew areas, it keeps Astronics in the conversation as airlines update cabins and as rivals like Safran, Collins Aerospace, and Thales also push passenger-experience hardware. Features such as backward compatibility with non-Qi2 devices, in-use status lights, and rapid module replacement in under three minutes are aimed at airlines’ two main concerns: aircraft downtime and maintenance complexity. Compliance with DO-160 and major aircraft original equipment manufacturer requirements, plus a 50,000 hour predicted mean time between failures, positions the product for use on a wide range of platforms if customers opt for it.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Astronics Narrative

  • The launch supports the existing narrative that Astronics is leaning into higher-margin cabin power and connectivity products that can benefit from aircraft production and retrofit cycles.
  • If the product requires meaningful upfront investment, or if adoption is slower than expected, it could temper some of the optimism around portfolio simplification and margin improvement.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on in-seat power leadership. Features like ultra-fast maintenance swaps and MagSafe-compatible alignment may not yet be fully reflected in expectations for recurring retrofit or aftermarket demand.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Astronics to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Astronics still carries a high level of debt, so product expansion like Qi2 needs to contribute to returns that justify the balance sheet risk.
  • ⚠️ Large one off items, such as past legal or restructuring charges, show that unexpected costs can affect reported results even when the product roadmap looks attractive.
  • 🎁 The company has been growing profit or revenue, and new cabin power products could support that trend if airlines and seat manufacturers adopt them.
  • 🎁 Analysts have flagged Astronics as trading below some fair value estimates, and a broader wireless charging offering may help support the view that the business has room to grow into that assessment.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how quickly the Qi2 Wireless Charging Module moves from product launch into real aircraft programs and retrofit orders, and whether airlines standardize on Astronics for both wired and wireless power. Watch for any commentary on pricing, margins, and certification progress with major aircraft manufacturers, as well as how the module features in discussions about backlog quality. Investors may also want to track competitive responses from other cabin-systems players and whether Astronics references Qi2 adoption in future updates on its in-seat power leadership.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Amgen (AMGN) After A 32% One-Year Share Price Rally?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Amgen’s share price still offers value after a strong run, this breakdown will help you weigh what you are really paying for.
  • At a last close of US$355.30, the stock has returned 1.2% over 7 days, 2.2% over 30 days, 8.4% year to date, 32.2% over 1 year, 60.1% over 3 years, and 61.7% over 5 years. These figures raise fair questions about what is already reflected in the price.
  • Recent attention around Amgen has focused on its role as a large US biotech, with investors watching how its pipeline and portfolio position it within the broader pharmaceuticals and biotech space. This context helps explain why sentiment around the stock and its valuation has been in focus.
  • Currently, Amgen scores a 4/6 valuation check score. The following sections will walk through different valuation methods to show what that number really means, and will finish with a way to put all these approaches into a clearer big picture.

Find out why Amgen's 32.2% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Amgen Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of the cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those projections back to today, to arrive at an estimate of what the entire business might be worth right now.

For Amgen, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $8.4b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest annual Free Cash Flow in the region of $13.6b in 2026, with projections rising to about $20.2b by 2035, with Simply Wall St extending estimates beyond the analyst window. These future cash flows are discounted back to today in $ to account for the time value of money and risk.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $638.49 per share for Amgen, compared with the recent share price of $355.30. That implies the shares trade at roughly a 44.4% discount to this DCF estimate, which indicates that the stock screens as undervalued under this method.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Amgen is undervalued by 44.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Amgen.

Approach 2: Amgen Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for what you are paying for each dollar of earnings, which makes it a common way to compare valuations across similar businesses.

A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market expects for future growth and how much risk investors are willing to accept. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” P/E, while lower growth or higher risk can justify a lower one.

Amgen currently trades on a P/E of 24.9x. That sits above the Biotechs industry average P/E of 17.1x, but below the broader peer group average of 38.4x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Amgen is 26.1x, which is its proprietary estimate of what Amgen’s P/E “should” be given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can offer a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it attempts to adjust for Amgen’s own growth profile and risk characteristics. With the Fair Ratio of 26.1x sitting slightly above the current 24.9x, the P/E suggests Amgen screens as undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

This is where Narratives come in as your way of attaching a clear story about Amgen to the numbers such as fair value, revenue, earnings and margin assumptions, and then seeing how that story plays out in a forecast.

These Narratives sit inside the Community page on Simply Wall St, are used by millions of investors, and are updated automatically when new information such as earnings, guidance or news is added. As a result, the numbers keep moving as the story changes.

Another investor might focus on risks and prefer a lower fair value around US$218.89 with flat or lower assumed revenues and a different P/E. Together, they frame a clear bull and bear range around what the stock might be worth, based on different assumptions for growth, margins and the P/E investors might be willing to pay in a few years time.

🐂 Amgen Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish Narrative: US$432.00 per share.

At a last close of US$355.30, that is about 17.7% below this Narrative fair value.

Revenue growth assumption used: 6.52% a year.

  • Assumes volume-led growth across around 15 products, with particular emphasis on obesity, rare disease and biosimilars supporting higher revenue and operating leverage.
  • Builds in higher profit margins over time, helped by AI-supported R&D, a broad late-stage pipeline in areas like obesity and oncology, and global expansion of biologics and biosimilars.
